varieties.name: 🧬 HYBRID = First-generation cross
varieties.origin: Native to Arabian Peninsula (Oman, UAE). Widely cultivated globally since 17th century.

varieties.name: Aloe barbadensis Miller (True Aloe Vera)
varieties.origin: Arabian Peninsula, cultivated since ancient times (2000+ years)
varieties.traits: Thick, fleshy, grey-green to green leaves in rosette formation, 50-100cm tall. White flecks may appear on young leaves but fade with maturity. Serrated margins with small white teeth. Stemless or very short-stemmed.
varieties.availability: Widely available throughout Brisbane nurseries, Bunnings, garden centers, and online marketplaces (Gumtree, eBay). Price range: $3-20 depending on size.

varieties.name: Aloe barbadensis 'Stockton'
varieties.origin: Selected variety of A. barbadensis Miller, identified by Rodney Stockton for superior medicinal qualities
varieties.traits: Pure green leaves without spots at maturity (young plants show white spots that fade). Slightly thicker and wider leaves at base than standard variety.

varieties.name: Aloe vera var. chinensis (Chinese Aloe, Spotted Aloe)
varieties.origin: Variety of A. barbadensis, cultivated extensively in China and India since ancient times
varieties.traits: Smaller than true aloe (up to 45cm). Grey-green leaves with persistent white spots that remain longer than standard variety. More compact, fan-shaped offsets. Leaves take on brownish-red hue in intense sun.
varieties.availability: Available in Brisbane but often not labeled separately from A. barbadensis. May be found at specialist succulent nurseries.

varieties.name: Aloe arborescens (Krantz Aloe, Candelabra Aloe)
varieties.origin: South Africa (Malawi, Mozambique, Zimbabwe). Cultivated globally since 1700s.
varieties.traits: Multi-headed branching shrub reaching 1.8-2.4m (6-8ft) tall. Narrow, tooth-edged leaves 15-22cm long in loose rosettes on woody stems. Grey-green foliage.
varieties.availability: Widely available in Brisbane at nurseries like Garden Express, Daleys Fruit, and local garden centers. Popular ornamental hedge plant.

varieties.name: Aloe arborescens 'Variegata'
varieties.origin: Cultivar of A. arborescens. Royal Horticultural Society Award of Garden Merit winner.
varieties.traits: Variegated leaves with cream/white striping. Same growth habit as species (1.8-2.4m tall).
varieties.availability: Specialty nurseries in Brisbane. Less common than standard green form.

varieties.name: Aloe ferox (Cape Aloe, Bitter Aloe)
varieties.origin: South Africa (Cape Province, Eastern Cape, KwaZulu-Natal). Cultivated for medicinal use since 1700s.
varieties.traits: Large single-stemmed tree aloe, 2-5m tall (occasionally 10ft). Thick, fleshy blue-green leaves in dense rosette. Reddish-brown spines on leaf margins and surfaces. Old dried leaves form 'petticoat' on stem.
varieties.availability: Available from Australian nurseries including The Seed Vine, Australian Seed, All Rare Herbs. Seeds and plants available online.

varieties.name: Aloe aristata (Lace Aloe, Torch Plant)
varieties.traits: Compact variety, 15-20cm tall. Small clump-forming rosettes with white speckles and 'lacy' white edges. Short, soft succulent leaves.
varieties.availability: Available from Brisbane nurseries and garden centers. Popular ornamental succulent.

varieties.name: Aloe barbadensis Miller (Ghrit Kumari, Kattar Vazha)
varieties.origin: Arabian Peninsula, introduced to India in 17th century. Now naturalized and cultivated across India for 300+ years.
varieties.traits: Grey-green fleshy leaves, 50-100cm tall. Stemless or short-stemmed rosettes. Common household and commercial cultivation variety.
varieties.availability: Widely available across Kerala. Found in every other household. Commercial plantings in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh. Available from Kerala Agricultural University, local nurseries, and farmers.

varieties.name: Aloe indica (Indian Aloe)
varieties.origin: Synonym/variety of A. barbadensis specific to Indian cultivation. Traditional Indian variety.
varieties.traits: Similar to A. barbadensis but adapted to Indian growing conditions over centuries. Grey-green leaves in rosette formation.

varieties.name: IC111271 (NBPGR Release)
varieties.origin: Released by National Botanical and Plant Genetic Resource (ICAR), New Delhi for Indian cultivation
varieties.traits: Selected variety of A. barbadensis with high therapeutic value. Improved variety for commercial cultivation.
varieties.availability: Available through Kerala Agricultural University, ICAR research centers, and certified nurseries. Recommended for commercial cultivation.

varieties.name: IC111269 (NBPGR Release)
varieties.traits: High-yielding variety selected for Indian growing conditions. Robust growth in tropical climates.
varieties.availability: Available from agricultural universities, ICAR centers, and government-approved nurseries in Kerala.

varieties.name: IC111280 (NBPGR Release)
varieties.origin: Released by NBPGR-ICAR for Indian commercial cultivation
varieties.traits: Selected for high biomass production and gel yield. Suitable for large-scale farming.
varieties.availability: Available through Kerala Agricultural University, agricultural research centers, and certified suppliers.

varieties.name: AL-1 (CIMAP Release)
varieties.origin: Released by Central Institute of Medicinal and Aromatic Plants (CIMAP), Lucknow
varieties.traits: Superior variety selected for medicinal properties and high gel content. Developed for Indian agroclimatic conditions.
varieties.availability: Available from CIMAP, Kerala Agricultural University, and research-affiliated nurseries. Recommended for commercial medicinal cultivation.

varieties.name: Aloe vera var. chinensis (Indian Spotted Aloe)
varieties.origin: Naturalized in India for centuries. Mentioned in ancient Chinese and Indian medicinal texts.
varieties.traits: Smaller variety with persistent white spots on grey-green leaves. Compact rosettes, more prolific offsetting than standard variety.
varieties.availability: Available in Kerala from local nurseries and home garden propagation. Often not distinguished from A. barbadensis in trade.

varieties.name: Aloe littoralis (Coastal Aloe)
varieties.origin: Coastal regions. Commercially important subspecies in India.
varieties.traits: Succulent rosette at top of upright, usually unbranched stem densely bearded with old dry leaves. Adapted to coastal sandy soils.
varieties.availability: Available in coastal Kerala regions. Found scattered along southern Indian coast. Local nurseries in coastal areas.

varieties.name: Aloe abyssinica (Abyssinian Aloe)
varieties.origin: Ethiopia, East Africa. Commercially important subspecies cultivated in India.
varieties.traits: Deep green leaves (often spotted when young), 1.5-2ft long, 3-4in broad. Densely rosulate, ensiform leaves. Marginal teeth large, deltoid, tipped with brown.
varieties.availability: Limited availability in Kerala. May be found through specialized medicinal plant nurseries or research institutions.

varieties.name: Aloe arborescens (Krantz Aloe)
varieties.origin: South Africa. Cultivated globally for medicinal and ornamental purposes.
varieties.traits: Large branching shrub, 1.8-2.4m tall. Multi-headed with narrow tooth-edged leaves in rosettes.
varieties.availability: Available in India from medicinal plant nurseries and botanical gardens. Growing popularity for medicinal gardens.
